section.title: Final Greetings
section.sectionRationale: The letter closes as it opened, with grace and a circle of names. Fellow prisoners and fellow workers send greetings, and Paul ends with the same grace of the Lord Jesus that framed the whole appeal, now prayed over the church's spirit.
section.thesis: Paul signs off surrounded by a company of fellow prisoners and workers and leaves the church with the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, the grace that alone makes the whole appeal for Onesimus possible.
section.frameNote: One movement: greetings from Paul's companions, and a closing blessing of grace on the church's spirit.

units.label: Greetings and closing grace
units.note: a company of witnesses and a final blessing

verses.text: Epaphras, my {{synaichmalotos|fellow prisoner}} in Christ Jesus, sends you greetings,
as do Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke, my {{synergos|fellow workers}}.
The {{charis|grace}} of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your {{pneuma|spirit}}.

synaichmalotos.gloss: A fellow prisoner or fellow captive, literally one taken captive alongside another, drawn from the language of prisoners of war. Epaphras shares Paul's imprisonment for the gospel, showing that Paul is not alone even in chains; the family of Christ stands with him.

synergos.gloss: A fellow worker or co-laborer, the same word Paul used of Philemon in verse 1. He now names Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke as his fellow workers, drawing Philemon into a wider fellowship of gospel partners and quietly reminding him that he too shares this labor of love.

charis.gloss: Grace, undeserved favor and kindness. The letter ends where it began, with grace, verse 3. This final grace is the power behind the whole appeal: only grace received can make a master welcome a runaway slave as a beloved brother.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, the inner person, here the seat of one's life before God. Paul prays grace upon your spirit, using a plural you that widens the blessing to the whole gathered church, so that all who heard the letter are left resting in the grace of Christ.

basic.context: Paul ends the letter the way he began it, with grace and with a group of names. Epaphras, who is in prison with him, sends greetings, as do Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke, Paul's fellow workers. Then Paul closes with a blessing: may the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit. The you here is plural, so the blessing reaches the whole church, not just Philemon.
basic.hermeneutics: Even the closing greetings do quiet work. By naming a circle of companions, Paul reminds Philemon that a whole gospel family is watching and standing with him, and that he belongs to something larger than his household. And by ending with grace, Paul leaves the deciding word not on duty but on the grace that makes obedience possible.
basic.originalLanguages: Epaphras is Paul's synaichmalotos, his fellow prisoner. Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke are his synergoi, fellow workers, the same word used of Philemon in verse 1. And the letter ends with charis, grace, upon your pneuma, your spirit, the plural you gathering the whole church into the blessing.
basic.typology: There is no formal type here, but the closing grace embodies the letter's theme. The grace that saves and reconciles, flowing from the Lord Jesus, is the same grace that must flow from Philemon to Onesimus, a small echo of the grace that flows from God to us and through us to others.
basic.christ: The last word is the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ. Everything the letter asks rests on Him: His grace saved Philemon, converted Onesimus, and alone can turn a master and a slave into brothers. Christ has the final word, as He has the first.
basic.god: God surrounds His servants with a family even in prison, and He leaves His people not with a burden but with grace. He is the God who gives what He commands, supplying by grace the very love and welcome He calls for.
basic.crossReferences: Colossians 4:10-14 lists the same companions, tying the two letters together. Galatians 6:18 ends with the same blessing of grace with your spirit. Second Timothy 4:10-11 later mentions Demas, Mark, and Luke again, with differing outcomes.
basic.oneStory: The letter ends on grace, and so does the Bible. The grace of the Lord Jesus is the thread from the first promise to the last page, the power that reconciles God to sinners and sinners to one another, and here it is prayed over a church learning to live out that reconciliation.

context.literary: The customary closing of an ancient letter: greetings from companions and a final benediction. Paul's mention of five named coworkers gives the private letter a public circle of witnesses, and the closing grace forms an inclusio with the opening greeting in verse 3, framing the whole appeal within grace.
context.historical: The same names appear in Colossians 4:10-14, sent from the same imprisonment, confirming that Philemon and Colossians travel together and belong to Paul's Roman captivity. Epaphras is the evangelist of Colossae; Mark is John Mark; Luke is the beloved physician; Demas is here a fellow worker, though 2 Timothy 4:10 later records his desertion. This company of witnesses adds gentle weight to Paul's appeal.
context.canonical: The closing anchors this personal letter within the wider apostolic mission and its network of coworkers. Ending on the grace of the Lord Jesus, it leaves the reader, like Philemon, resting the whole matter of reconciliation on grace rather than on mere obligation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To close warmly, to remind Philemon that a company of gospel workers stands with Paul and watches with love, and to leave the church resting in the grace of Christ, the grace that makes the requested reconciliation possible.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The greetings describe Paul's fellowship of coworkers; they do not prescribe a rule. The abiding note is the benediction of grace, the reminder that the Christian life, including its hardest obediences, is lived from and toward the grace of the Lord Jesus.

debates.question: Why does Paul name so many companions in such a short, personal letter?

views.label: To surround the appeal with loving witnesses
views.case: Naming five coworkers reminds Philemon that his response will be known and shared by a wider gospel family, adding warm accountability to the private request.

views.label: To express genuine fellowship and greeting
views.case: These are real friends who truly send greetings; the closing simply reflects the deep, communal life Paul shared with his coworkers, even in prison.

debates.bearing: Both are true and reinforce each other: the letter is personal yet never merely private, and Philemon is drawn to act as part of a watching, loving community rather than in isolation.

study.originalLanguages: The closing quietly gathers the letter's people. Epaphras is Paul's synaichmalotos, a fellow prisoner taken captive alongside him for Christ. Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke are his synergoi, fellow workers, the very title given to Philemon in verse 1, so Philemon is numbered among them. And the final word is charis, grace, prayed upon your pneuma, your spirit, with a plural you that opens the blessing to the whole church.

typology.type: The closing grace of the Lord Jesus Christ
typology.fulfillment: An echo of the gospel itself: grace flowing from Christ to His people and, through them, to one another, the very movement the letter asks of Philemon toward Onesimus.

study.christ: The letter's last word is the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and everything rests on Him. His grace had saved Philemon, His grace had found and changed Onesimus in a prison cell, and His grace alone could make a master and a runaway slave into brothers. Paul does not close with pressure but with Christ, leaving the outcome in the hands of the One whose grace supplies whatever He asks. Christ has the final word, as He had the first.
study.god: God does not leave His servants alone; even in chains Paul is surrounded by a family of fellow prisoners and workers, a picture of the fellowship God gives His people. And God sends His people out not under a weight of bare duty but under grace, for He is the God who gives what He commands, providing by His grace the very love and welcome that He calls His people to show.

crossReferences.note: The same circle of companions, Epaphras, Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke, sent from the same imprisonment, linking Philemon and Colossians.
Paul ends with the same benediction, the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit.
Demas, Mark, and Luke appear again later, with Demas having deserted, a sober reminder that present fellowship must be kept.
Epaphras is the faithful minister who brought the gospel to Colossae, now a fellow prisoner with Paul.

study.oneStory: The letter ends on grace, and so does the whole Bible. The grace of the Lord Jesus is the thread from the first promise in Eden to the last page of Revelation, the power that reconciles God to sinners and sinners to one another. Here that grace is prayed over a church learning to live out reconciliation in the hardest of cases, and it sends them, and us, to pass on the grace we have received.

sermon.title: Refresh My Heart
sermon.why: The closing message of Begg's series A Study in Philemon covers Philemon 1:17-25, including these final greetings, and draws the letter to a close on the grace of the Lord Jesus and the fellowship of the gospel. A faithful, orthodox handling of this movement.

quiz.q: Who does Paul describe as his fellow prisoner in these closing verses?

options.text: Epaphras, his fellow prisoner in Christ Jesus.
options.note: Epaphras, the evangelist of Colossae, shares Paul's imprisonment, verse 23.

options.text: Aristarchus, one of his named companions.
options.note: In Colossians 4:10 Aristarchus is called Paul's fellow prisoner, but in Philemon that title is given to Epaphras.
Luke is named among the fellow workers, not as the fellow prisoner here.
Timothy is named in the opening greeting, not here as fellow prisoner.

quiz.q: How does Paul end the letter?

options.text: With a blessing: the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit.
options.note: The letter closes on grace, framing the whole appeal, verse 25.

options.text: With warm greetings from his companions and a final blessing.
options.note: The greetings do come just before, but the letter's final words are the blessing of grace.

options.text: With a stern final warning to obey.
options.note: Paul closes with grace, not with a threat.
Paul ends with the grace of Christ, not a financial appeal.

quiz.q: Why does Paul name a circle of coworkers in such a personal letter?

options.text: To surround the appeal with loving witnesses and remind Philemon he belongs to a wider gospel family.
options.note: The named companions add warm accountability and fellowship to the private request.

options.text: To pass along the genuine greetings of real friends in the faith.
options.note: These are real friends sending real greetings, and their inclusion also lends warm accountability to the appeal.

options.text: To fill space at the end of the letter.
options.note: The greetings are meaningful, not filler; they connect Philemon to a watching family.

options.text: To boast about how many friends he has.
options.note: The point is genuine fellowship and witness, not boasting.

quiz.q: What is the significance of ending the letter with grace?

options.text: Grace is the power behind the whole appeal; only grace received can make Philemon welcome Onesimus as a brother.
options.note: The letter opens and closes on grace, resting the outcome on Christ rather than duty.

options.text: It is only a standard sign-off with no real meaning.
options.note: It is Paul's customary close, but here it carries the whole weight of the appeal.

options.text: It means Philemon is free to ignore the request.
options.note: Grace enables the obedience Paul expects; it does not cancel it.

options.text: It shows Paul had run out of things to say.
options.note: The closing grace is a deliberate and fitting frame for the letter.

questions: Even in prison Paul was surrounded by a family of fellow workers. Who are the companions God has given you for the long road of following Christ, and are you keeping those bonds?
Paul left Philemon not with pressure but with grace. When you face a hard obedience, do you draw on grace, or try to grit it out on duty alone?
The same grace that saved Philemon was the grace he had to pass on to Onesimus. Where is God asking you to extend to someone the grace you yourself have received?
Demas is a fellow worker here but later deserted Paul. What helps you keep going faithfully rather than drifting away over time?
The letter begins and ends with grace. How would your relationships change if grace framed both the start and the finish of your dealings with others?

translationNotes.note: Fellow prisoner renders synaichmalotos, literally a fellow captive of war; Epaphras shared Paul's imprisonment for the gospel.
The same companions, Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke, are named in Colossians 4:10-14, sent from the same imprisonment.
Your spirit uses a plural you, widening the closing blessing to the whole gathered church. Some manuscripts, BYZ and TR, add Amen, as the BSB footnote notes.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Philemon 1:23-25. No em dashes appear in this movement's text. BYZ and TR add Amen at the end of verse 25, per the BSB footnote; the BSB main text omits it and is followed here.
